behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is incomplete taxonomy list in the API request payload. This is testable by mocking the API client and inspecting the exact arguments passed—no real I/O, database, or subprocess needed. A mock that captures call arguments will reveal if labels are missing.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client. Call the classification module with a test document. Inspect the mock's call arguments and verify that the complete taxonomy labels list is present in the request payload sent to the API.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is validation bypass—invalid labels propagate to the caller. This is caught by a Unit test: mock the API to return an out-of-taxonomy label and verify the response validation logic rejects it.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client to return a classification label not in the configured taxonomy. Call the module and verify it returns 'unknown' instead of the invalid label.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: The primary failure mode is missing or broken caching logic. A Unit test mocking the API can verify that identical inputs trigger a cache hit (API called once, not twice). Full verification with a real non-deterministic API would require Agentic testing, but Unit testing captures whether caching is implemented.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client to return consistent results. Call the module twice with the same document. Verify that (1) both results are identical and (2) the underlying API was called exactly once on the second call (cache hit).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit testing verifies caching logic. True end-to-end behavior with a real non-deterministic LLM would require Agentic testing to ensure consistency actually holds in production.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is missing or broken budget enforcement. This is testable at Unit level: mock the API to return specific token counts, verify the module enforces the limit (accepts under budget, rejects or degrades over budget).
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client to return token usage information. Test with a token count within budget and verify acceptance. Test with a count exceeding budget and verify the module either rejects the request or applies the configured cost-control logic.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is missing retry logic on rate-limit errors. This is testable at Unit level: mock the API to raise a rate-limit error on first call and succeed on second, mock time.sleep to prevent slow tests, and verify the module retries and succeeds. No real network, database, or subprocess needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client to raise a rate-limit error on the first call and return success on the second. Mock time.sleep (or asyncio.sleep) to avoid test slowdown. Call the module and verify it handles the rate-limit gracefully, retries, and returns a successful result without propagating an error to the caller.
